svg.text: FITing-Tree vs. PGM-Index — routing the same query
Searching for key k = 76 (ε = 1). Same data, same leaf segments — the difference is everything above the leaves.
B+-tree on top · learned step only at the leaf
segments all the way down · learned step at every level
final binary search: A[16..18] within ±ε → found at position 18
Anatomy: what's actually inside each node
route: compare k to separators → follow a pointer
each segment's first-key + a pointer to it.
still just keys + pointers — the bottom of the tree
③ Segment — the learned part, a separate object the leaf points at
predict: pos = (k − startKey)·slope → binary-search ±ε in data. The green buffer absorbs inserts (delta strategy).
→ Two different kinds of node (B+-tree node vs. segment) stitched together — the "mixes traditional + learned" design.
Every node — root, internal, and leaf — is the same triple:
Two floats + one key. That is the entire node.
No child pointers, no separators. The next level is found by computing fs(k), then binary-searching a 2ε window —
navigation is arithmetic, not a stored pointer.
→ One uniform learned object at every level → a "pure learned index."
A B+-tree node spends real bytes on separator keys and child pointers at every level — and FITing-Tree hangs a separate slope/dataPtr/buffer
segment object below the tree. A PGM node is just (key, slope, intercept) — two floats and a key — and recomputes navigation instead of storing pointers.
That uniformity is a big part of why PGM is smaller, and why it can call itself "fully learned": one kind of object, top to bottom — no traditional structure anywhere.
Both diagrams share the same leaf segments and the same final search window — only the path down to it, and what each node stores, differs.
